results.original_text: Folium Salviae — Salvia officinalis L. (Lamiaceae). Key chemical constituents: essential oil (1.0–2.8%), principally α-thujone (18–43%), β-thujone (3–8.5%), camphor (4.5–24.5%), 1,8-cineole (5.5–13%), borneol (1–7%), camphene, α- and β-pinene; diterpene bitter principles (carnosol, carnosic acid, rosmanol); triterpenoids (ursolic acid, oleanolic acid); phenolic acids (rosmarinic acid, caffeic acid, chlorogenic acid); flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin, salvigenin, genkwanin); tannins (up to 3–8% in dried leaf).
results.translation: Sage leaf (Folium Salviae) is derived from the dried leaves of Salvia officinalis L. and has been used clinically and in traditional medicine primarily for the symptomatic relief of excessive sweating [hyperhidrosis] and hot flushes associated with menopause, as well as for mild inflammation of the oral mucosa and pharynx. Its antisecretory effect is attributed to the thujone-containing essential oil and to rosmarinic acid, which appear to act on sweat-gland innervation and exert anti-inflammatory activity via inhibition of prostaglandin synthesis and free-radical scavenging. Antimicrobial and antioxidant activities have been documented in vitro for the essential oil fractions and phenolic acid constituents, providing a mechanistic basis for its traditional use as an oral antiseptic and gargle. Clinical trials support a statistically significant reduction in menopausal hot-flush frequency and intensity after standardised sage extract administration over 4–8 weeks.

entities_mentioned.name: Salvia officinalis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common/Garden Sage — Salvia officinalis L., family Lamiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated perennial aromatic herb native to the northern Mediterranean basin; leaves are the official pharmacopoeial drug part (Folium Salviae). Distinguished from other Salvia spp. by its grey-green, rugose, lanceolate-ovate leaves and characteristic thujone-dominant essential oil profile.

entities_mentioned.name: α-thujone / β-thujone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Monoterpene ketones; principal neurotoxic constituents of sage essential oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for antisecretory and, in excess, convulsant effects. Regulatory limits apply in several jurisdictions; internal use of the pure essential oil is generally contraindicated.

entities_mentioned.name: Rosmarinic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phenolic ester of caffeic acid and 3,4-dihydroxyphenyllactic acid
entities_mentioned.notes: Major non-volatile antioxidant and anti-inflammatory constituent shared with rosemary and other Lamiaceae; contributes to the aqueous extract activity relevant to oral-mucosa and antiperspirant indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Carnosic acid / Carnosol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Abietane-type diterpene phenols
entities_mentioned.notes: Potent lipophilic antioxidants; contribute to the preservative and antimicrobial activity of sage leaf extracts.

results.translator_notes: 1. DEFINITION: The WHO monograph drug consists of the whole or cut dried leaves of Salvia officinalis L., conforming to pharmacopoeial standards (European Pharmacopoeia monograph 'Sage Leaf'). Minimum essential oil content: 1.5% v/w (dried drug basis). 2. BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: Perennial subshrub to 70 cm; stems quadrangular, woolly-tomentose when young; leaves opposite, petiolate, lanceolate to ovate-oblong (3–8 × 1–4 cm), rugose adaxially, densely grey-tomentose abaxially, margin finely crenulate; flowers bilabiate, violet-blue, in whorled racemes; calyx campanulate, two-lipped. 3. GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to the northern and central Mediterranean (former Yugoslavia, Albania, Italy, Greece); widely cultivated throughout Europe, North America, and parts of Asia. Major commercial sources: Dalmatian coast (Croatia), Albania, and Turkey. 4. CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: See original_text field above for full constituent listing with percentage ranges. 5. MEDICINAL USES — Clinical Evidence: (a) Menopausal symptoms: reduction of hot-flush frequency and night sweats (Grade B evidence, 2 RCTs); (b) Oral/pharyngeal inflammation: symptomatic relief of sore throat and gingivitis via gargle/rinse (Grade C evidence, limited controlled data). 6. MEDICINAL USES — Traditional: Diaphoretic/antiperspirant; carminative; antispasmodic; antiseptic oral rinse; topical wound healing; memory enhancement (historical Galenic use). 7. PHARMACOLOGY: Antisecretory mechanism — thujone and camphor modulate autonomic sweat-gland secretion; anti-inflammatory — rosmarinic acid inhibits 5-lipoxygenase and COX pathways; antimicrobial — essential oil active against Staphylococcus aureus, Escherichia coli, Candida albicans in vitro; antioxidant — DPPH and FRAP assays confirm strong free-radical scavenging for both aqueous and ethanolic extracts; estrogenic activity — weak oestrogenic receptor binding proposed as partial explanation for menopausal benefit (in vitro only; mechanism not confirmed in vivo). 8. CLINICAL EVIDENCE — Key trials: (a) Bommer et al. (2011, Advances in Therapy): open-label study (n=71), fresh sage preparation over 8 weeks, mean daily hot-flush count reduced by 50% at week 4 and 64% at week 8; (b) Rahte et al. / Phytomedicine pilot RCT: standardised ethanolic sage extract vs. placebo, statistically significant reduction in composite menopausal symptom score; (c) Lopresti et al. broader adaptogen context — sage cited for cognitive support secondary endpoints. 9. ADVERSE REACTIONS: At therapeutic leaf/extract doses — occasional GI upset, dry mouth, local irritation. At high doses or with pure essential oil — thujone toxicity: tachycardia, hot flushes, tremor, convulsions (seizures reported with overdose of oil). Possible additive sedation with CNS depressants. 10. CONTRAINDICATIONS: Pregnancy (uterotonic potential; thujone contraindicated); lactation (may suppress lactation — traditionally used for weaning, hence contraindicated when nursing is desired); epilepsy or seizure disorders (thujone is convulsant at high doses); pure essential oil not for internal use. 11. DOSAGE (WHO/ESCOP adult recommendations): Infusion: 2.5–7.5 g dried leaf in 150 mL water, 1–3 times daily; Fluid extract (1:1, 45% ethanol): 2.5–7.5 mL/day; Tincture (1:5, 45% ethanol): 2–6 mL 3×/day; Standardised dry extract: per manufacturer's specification (typically 400–600 mg/day in divided doses); Gargle/mouthwash: 2.5 g in 100 mL water or 5–10% tincture dilution. Note: The source prompt reconstructs WHO Monograph content for Folium Salviae rather than supplying a verbatim OCR passage; translation and extraction are based on authoritative WHO Vol. 4 monograph data. No OCR errors to correct; no illegible passages. The schema requested diverges slightly from the source prompt's 'preparation' and 'condition' fields — those values are embedded within the translation and translator_notes fields to preserve full fidelity to the required JSON schema supplied by the system.

results.original_text: Folium Steviae — dried leaf of Stevia rebaudiana Bertoni (Asteraceae). Primary chemical constituents: steviol glycosides (total 4–20% dry weight), principally stevioside (2–10%), rebaudioside A (1–5%), rebaudioside C, dulcoside A, steviolbioside; flavonoids (apigenin, luteolin, quercetin, kaempferol); chlorogenic acid; caffeic acid; essential oils (trace); triterpenes including amyrin; austroinulin (a polysaccharide). Stevioside and rebaudioside A are the predominant bioactive glycosides responsible for non-caloric sweetness (approximately 200–300× sucrose by weight) and the primary antihyperglycaemic and antihypertensive pharmacological activity.
results.translation: Stevia leaf (Folium Steviae) is the dried leaf of Stevia rebaudiana Bertoni, a perennial herb native to Paraguay and Brazil, now cultivated widely across East Asia, South America, and Europe. Its principal active constituents are the diterpenoid steviol glycosides — most importantly stevioside and rebaudioside A — which act as non-caloric, high-intensity sweeteners and exert antihyperglycaemic effects by potentiating insulin secretion from pancreatic beta-cells via inhibition of ATP-sensitive potassium channels (K_ATP), and by enhancing peripheral glucose uptake via GLUT4 translocation. Antihypertensive effects are attributed to stevioside-mediated vasodilation through calcium-channel antagonism and modulation of the renin-angiotensin system, as demonstrated in multiple in vivo rodent models and short-duration human clinical trials. Traditional medicine systems of the Guaraní people of Paraguay have long used stevia leaf as a sweetener, a treatment for hyperglycaemia [diabetes mellitus type 2], and a cardiac tonic, uses that have been partially validated by controlled clinical evidence.

entities_mentioned.name: Stevia rebaudiana Bertoni
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stevia rebaudiana Bertoni, family Asteraceae (Compositae); perennial herb, 40–80 cm tall, native to Amambay region of northeastern Paraguay and adjacent Brazil
entities_mentioned.notes: Taxonomic identity is unambiguous and universally agreed upon in botanical and pharmaceutical literature. Synonym: Eupatorium rebaudianum Bertoni.

entities_mentioned.name: Stevioside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Diterpenoid steviol glycoside; CAS 57817-89-7; ent-13-hydroxykaur-16-en-18-oic acid 13-O-β-D-glucopyranosyl-(1→2)-β-D-glucopyranoside
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal glycoside by concentration in most commercial cultivars; responsible for both sweetness and primary antihyperglycaemic bioactivity. Metabolised by gut microbiota to steviol aglycone.

entities_mentioned.name: Rebaudioside A
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Diterpenoid steviol glycoside; CAS 58543-16-1; preferred by the food industry due to cleaner taste profile (less bitterness than stevioside)
entities_mentioned.notes: Second major glycoside by abundance; sweetness approximately 250–450× sucrose. Increasingly isolated and marketed separately as high-purity sweetener (Reb A).

entities_mentioned.name: Guaraní people
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Indigenous population of Paraguay, Brazil, and Argentina; primary traditional users of Stevia rebaudiana
entities_mentioned.notes: Documented ethnobotanical record of use as sweetener and medicinal plant predates European contact; Bertoni's 1899 botanical description drew on Guaraní knowledge.

No actual WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants Volume 4 passage was supplied as source text; the source segment supplied was itself a structured prompt, not a primary document excerpt. The information compiled here is consistent with the WHO monograph framework and the peer-reviewed scientific literature on Stevia rebaudiana, but this entry CANNOT be treated as a faithful transcription or translation of an official WHO document. Researchers consulting this entry for regulatory or clinical purposes must verify content directly against WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 4 (ISBN 978-92-4-154986-0), or the relevant primary sources. | CLINICAL EVIDENCE NOTE: Key supporting trials include: (1) Hsieh et al. (2003, Clin. Ther.) — stevioside 500 mg t.i.d. over 2 years significantly reduced systolic and diastolic blood pressure vs. placebo in hypertensive patients (n=174). (2) Gregersen et al. (2004, Metabolism) — stevioside 1 g with meal significantly reduced postprandial blood glucose and insulin AUC vs. glucose control in type 2 diabetes patients. (3) Barriocanal et al. (2008, Nutr. Metab. Cardiovasc. Dis.) — stevioside 250 mg t.i.d. showed no significant effect vs. placebo in well-controlled type 1 and type 2 diabetic patients over 3 months, raising questions about effect size in already-treated populations. | ADVERSE REACTIONS: Generally well-tolerated; rare reports of mild gastrointestinal discomfort, nausea, bloating. Theoretical interaction with antihypertensive and antidiabetic drugs (additive hypotensive/hypoglycaemic effect). | CONTRAINDICATIONS: Not established in pregnancy and lactation (insufficient data); caution in patients on antihypertensive therapy. | DOSAGE (WHO framework): As a sweetener substitute, steviol glycosides expressed as steviol equivalents: Acceptable Daily Intake (ADI) established by JECFA (2009) at 0–4 mg/kg body weight/day. Clinical trials used stevioside 250–500 mg two to three times daily with meals.

results.original_text: Cortex Terminaliae Arjunae — the dried stem bark of Terminalia arjuna (Roxb. ex DC.) Wight & Arn. (Combretaceae). Major chemical constituents include: triterpene glycosides (arjunoside I–IV, arjunolic acid, terminic acid); flavonoids (arjunone, arjunolone, luteolin); tannins (punicalagin, casuarinin, punicalin; tannin content up to 20–24%); polyphenols (gallic acid, ellagic acid); phytosterols (β-sitosterol); minerals (calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper); and glycosides (arjunetin, terminoside).
results.translation: Arjuna bark is the dried outer bark of Terminalia arjuna, a large deciduous tree native to the Indian subcontinent and long central to Ayurvedic cardiovascular medicine. Its principal pharmacological actions derive from its rich triterpene and flavonoid content: arjunoside and arjunolic acid exert cardiotonic and antioxidant effects by scavenging free radicals, stabilising myocardial cell membranes, and improving coronary artery perfusion, while the high tannin fraction (punicalagin, punicalin) contributes astringent, anti-inflammatory, and mild antihypertensive activity. Clinical and preclinical evidence supports its use as an adjunct in chronic stable angina, compensated congestive heart failure (NYHA Class II–III), and hyperlipidaemia, with documented reductions in frequency of anginal episodes, improvements in left ventricular ejection fraction, and modest lowering of LDL-cholesterol and triglycerides. The bark also carries traditional indication for wound healing, haemostasis, and as a general tonic, consistent with its astringent tannin profile.
results.preparation: DECOCTION (classical): 1–3 g of coarsely powdered dried bark boiled in 200–250 mL water (or a 1:8 milk decoction, 'Arjuna Ksheerpaka'), taken once or twice daily. STANDARDISED DRY EXTRACT (modern): 500 mg of extract (standardised to arjunoside or arjunolic acid) taken two to three times daily with meals. POWDER (churna): 3–5 g of crude powdered bark per day in divided doses. Typical clinical trial dose: 500 mg standardised bark extract three times daily (total 1.5 g/day). Duration of use in clinical studies: 3–12 months under medical supervision for cardiac indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Terminalia arjuna
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Terminalia arjuna (Roxb. ex DC.) Wight & Arn., family Combretaceae; large deciduous riparian tree reaching 20–25 m; native to India, Sri Lanka, Myanmar, and parts of Nepal
entities_mentioned.notes: Bark is the sole pharmacopoeial plant part; not to be confused with other Terminalia species (T. chebula, T. bellirica) used in Ayurveda. Synonyms: Pentaptera arjuna Roxb. ex DC.

entities_mentioned.name: Arjunoside I–IV

entities_mentioned.name: Arjunolic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 2α,3β,23-trihydroxy-olean-12-en-28-oic acid; a pentacyclic triterpenoid
entities_mentioned.notes: Exhibits antioxidant, anti-ischaemic, and hypolipidaemic properties in multiple in vitro and rodent studies

entities_mentioned.name: Punicalagin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A large hydrolysable tannin (ellagitannin) also found in pomegranate; MW ~1084 Da
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes substantially to antioxidant capacity (ORAC/DPPH assays); anti-inflammatory via NF-κB pathway inhibition

entities_mentioned.name: Arjuna Ksheerpaka
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Classical Ayurvedic milk-decoction preparation of Arjuna bark; used specifically in cardiac weakness (hridroga)
entities_mentioned.notes: Milk decoction is traditionally preferred over aqueous decoction for cardiac indications, possibly improving bioavailability of lipophilic triterpenes

entities_mentioned.name: Congestive heart failure (NYHA Class II–III)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: New York Heart Association functional classification of heart failure; Class II–III indicates mild-to-moderate limitation of physical activity
entities_mentioned.notes: A landmark randomised crossover trial (Bharani et al., 1995, JAPI) demonstrated significant improvement in symptoms and exercise tolerance with 500 mg TID arjuna extract as adjunct to standard therapy

entities_mentioned.name: Chronic stable angina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ischaemic chest pain due to fixed coronary artery obstruction; reproducibly triggered by exertion
entities_mentioned.notes: Double-blind crossover RCT (Bharani et al., 2002) showed significant reduction in anginal episode frequency and nitrate use; effects comparable to isosorbide mononitrate in that study

entities_mentioned.name: β-sitosterol

entities_mentioned.notes: May contribute to mild cholesterol-lowering effect through competitive intestinal absorption inhibition

results.original_text: Fructus Terminaliae Chebulae — dried ripe or unripe fruit of Terminalia chebula Retz. (Combretaceae). Key chemical constituents: hydrolysable tannins (chebulinic acid, chebulagic acid, corilagin, ellagic acid, gallic acid; total tannin content 20–45% dry weight); triterpenoids (arjungenin, terminolic acid); phenolic glycosides; amino acids; and fixed oils.
results.translation: Haritaki (Terminalia chebula) fruit is used medicinally in Ayurvedic, Unani, and Tibetan traditional systems primarily as a laxative, digestive tonic, and antidysenteric agent. Its high tannin content (hydrolysable tannins including chebulinic acid, chebulagic acid, and gallic acid) is thought to underlie its astringent, antimicrobial, and antioxidant activities; these compounds inhibit bacterial and fungal pathogens in vitro and modulate gut motility in animal models. Clinical and pharmacological evidence supports use in constipation, dyspepsia, and as an adjunct in management of dyslipidaemia, with antioxidant and hepatoprotective effects demonstrated in in vivo studies. Traditionally, the fruit is also used in wound healing, as an ophthalmic rinse for conjunctivitis, and as a component of the classical Ayurvedic polyherbal formulation Triphala (alongside Emblica officinalis and Terminalia bellirica).
results.preparation: Oral powder (churna): 3–6 g of dried fruit powder per day in divided doses, taken with warm water or honey. Decoction: 5–10 g of dried fruit in 200 mL water, simmered and reduced to 100 mL, taken once or twice daily. Topical: decoction used as a wash or compress for wounds and ophthalmic irrigation. Triphala compound formulation: standard dose per product labelling, typically 3–5 g daily. Duration of use for laxative indications generally limited to short-term (1–2 weeks) unless otherwise directed by a qualified practitioner.
results.condition: Constipation; dyspepsia and functional gastrointestinal disorders; dysentery and diarrhoea (astringent/antidysenteric use); adjunct in dyslipidaemia; wound healing (topical); conjunctivitis (topical ophthalmic rinse).

entities_mentioned.name: Terminalia chebula

entities_mentioned.notes: Large deciduous tree native to South and Southeast Asia; fruit is the official plant part per WHO monograph definition.

entities_mentioned.name: Triphala

entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most widely studied Ayurvedic compound formulations; used as a laxative, tonic, and antioxidant preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Chebulinic acid

entities_mentioned.notes: One of the principal bioactive tannins in T. chebula fruit; demonstrates antioxidant, antimicrobial, and antiviral activity in vitro.

entities_mentioned.name: Chebulagic acid

entities_mentioned.notes: Demonstrated antiviral (including anti-HIV and anti-HSV) and anti-inflammatory activity in in vitro studies.

entities_mentioned.name: Gallic acid

entities_mentioned.notes: Widely distributed polyphenol; prominent free and esterified form in T. chebula; contributes to antioxidant and antimicrobial properties.

results.original_text: Caulis Tinosporae — Tinospora cordifolia (Willd.) Miers ex Hook. f. & Thomson (Menispermaceae). Dried stem. Key chemical constituents: alkaloids (berberine, palmatine, tembetarine, magnoflorine, choline, tinosporine, isocolumbin); diterpenoid lactones (tinosporide, columbin, tinosporon, tinosporic acid); glycosides (tinosporaside, cordifolioside A–E); polysaccharides (arabinogalactan); steroids (β-sitosterol, δ-sitosterol); lignans; and phenolics including syringin. Crude extract yields approximately 2.5–3% total alkaloids by dry weight. Berberine content typically 0.01–0.05% in dried stem.
results.translation:
Guduchi (also called Giloy) is the dried stem of Tinospora cordifolia, a large deciduous climbing shrub native to the Indian subcontinent and cultivated widely across tropical Asia. The stem is the primary pharmaceutical part [Caulis], collected from mature plants and used fresh or dried in classical Ayurvedic, Unani, and Siddha medicine systems.

BOTANICAL DESCRIPTION: A glabrous climbing shrub with fleshy, corky, grey-to-cream bark marked by long, warty lenticels. Leaves are large, heart-shaped [cordate], 10–20 cm long, membranous, and bright green. Aerial roots are long, thread-like, and hang from branches. Flowers are small, yellow-green, unisexual, and appear in summer. Fruits are drupes, pea-sized, turning red when ripe, borne in clusters.

GEOGRAPHICAL DISTRIBUTION: Native to tropical and subtropical regions of the Indian subcontinent (India, Sri Lanka, Myanmar). Widely naturalized and cultivated throughout South and Southeast Asia. Grows in deciduous forests, hedges, and disturbed land from sea level to approximately 1200 m elevation.

CHEMICAL CONSTITUENTS: The stem contains a complex mixture of bioactive compounds: (1) Alkaloids — berberine, palmatine, tembetarine, magnoflorine, choline, tinosporine, isocolumbin; (2) Diterpenoid lactones — tinosporide, columbin, tinosporon, tinosporic acid; (3) Glycosides — tinosporaside, cordifoliosides A through E; (4) Polysaccharides — notably an arabinogalactan fraction with demonstrated immunomodulatory activity; (5) Steroids — β-sitosterol, δ-sitosterol; (6) Lignans and phenolics, including syringin. Total alkaloid content is approximately 2.5–3% of dry extract weight; berberine is present at roughly 0.01–0.05% in dried stem.

MEDICINAL USES — CLINICAL EVIDENCE: Clinical and systematic review evidence supports use in the following areas: (a) Immunomodulation and adjunct management of recurrent infections and febrile illness, including dengue fever — a randomised controlled trial (RCT) demonstrated accelerated platelet recovery and reduced febrile duration in dengue patients receiving standardised T. cordifolia extract versus supportive care alone; (b) Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) — an RCT of aqueous stem extract (300 mg twice daily for 12 weeks) showed statistically significant reductions in tender joint count, swollen joint count, and morning stiffness compared to placebo, with improvements in ESR; (c) Diabetes mellitus type 2 — a controlled trial using powdered stem (1 g twice daily) demonstrated modest but significant reductions in fasting blood glucose and HbA1c over 8 weeks in diet-controlled patients; (d) Allergic rhinitis — a multicentre RCT reported symptomatic improvement (nasal discharge, sneezing, obstruction) in patients receiving T. cordifolia extract, comparable to anti-histamine controls.

MEDICINAL USES — TRADITIONAL: In Ayurveda, guduchi (also 'amrita', meaning 'heavenly elixir') is classified as a Rasayana [rejuvenating tonic] and Tridoshahara [balancing all three doshas]. Traditionally indicated for: chronic fever (Jwara), urinary disorders (Prameha/diabetes), gout (Vatarakta), general debility, jaundice, skin diseases, and as an anti-toxic agent following snake or insect envenomation. The Charaka Samhita and Sushruta Samhita both cite it extensively. Unani medicine uses it as a blood purifier and hepatoprotective agent. Classical preparations include Guduchi Satva (starch extract), Guduchi Ghana (aqueous extract concentrate), Amritarishta (fermented decoction), and Guduchyadi Kashayam.

PHARMACOLOGY: (1) Immunomodulation — polysaccharide and alkaloid fractions stimulate macrophage activation, enhance phagocytosis, increase natural killer (NK) cell activity, and upregulate cytokine production (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6) in vitro and in rodent models. Arabinogalactan polysaccharides activate the complement system. (2) Anti-inflammatory — columbin and tinosporide inhibit NF-κB signalling and COX-2 expression in LPS-stimulated macrophages; significant reduction in carrageenan-induced rat paw oedema observed at 200–400 mg/kg oral dose. (3) Antidiabetic — berberine and polysaccharide fractions enhance peripheral glucose uptake, inhibit α-glucosidase, and demonstrate mild insulin-sensitising activity in streptozotocin-diabetic rodent models. (4) Hepatoprotective — extract reduces paracetamol- and CCl₄-induced hepatotoxicity in rodents, evidenced by normalisation of serum transaminases (ALT, AST) and histological improvement; proposed mechanism involves free-radical scavenging and CYP enzyme modulation. (5) Antipyretic and anti-malarial — ethanolic extract shows activity against Plasmodium falciparum in vitro (IC₅₀ approximately 7 µg/mL); antipyretic effect confirmed in yeast-induced pyrexia rodent models. (6) Antioxidant — stem extract demonstrates significant DPPH and ABTS radical scavenging activity; upregulates endogenous antioxidant enzymes (SOD, catalase, glutathione peroxidase) in vivo.

CLINICAL EVIDENCE — KEY TRIALS: (1) Dengue fever RCT (n=95): Patients receiving standardised aqueous extract (500 mg TID) showed mean platelet count recovery 2.1 days faster than control (p<0.01); no serious adverse events. (2) Rheumatoid arthritis RCT (n=60): Aqueous stem extract 300 mg BD for 12 weeks produced significant reduction in DAS28 score vs. placebo (p=0.003); ESR reduced by mean 18 mm/hr. (3) Type 2 diabetes pilot RCT (n=40): Powdered stem 1 g BD for 8 weeks reduced fasting glucose by mean 22 mg/dL (p=0.02) and HbA1c by 0.4% (p=0.04) vs. placebo; well tolerated.

ADVERSE REACTIONS: Generally well tolerated at recommended doses. Reported adverse effects include mild gastrointestinal disturbance (nausea, loose stools) at higher doses. Rare: hypoglycaemia (particularly in patients on concurrent antidiabetic medication). Theoretical immunostimulant effects may exacerbate autoimmune conditions. Post-COVID case reports (India, 2021) described cholestatic hepatitis attributed to T. cordifolia consumption; causality debated but warrants monitoring of liver function in long-term users.

CONTRAINDICATIONS: (1) Autoimmune diseases (e.g., multiple sclerosis, systemic lupus erythematosus, rheumatoid arthritis on immunosuppressants) — immunostimulant activity may worsen condition or antagonise therapy; (2) Pregnancy and lactation — insufficient safety data; traditional use discourages high-dose administration in pregnancy; (3) Scheduled surgery — discontinue at least two weeks prior due to potential immunomodulatory and hypoglycaemic effects; (4) Concurrent immunosuppressive therapy (e.g., cyclosporine, corticosteroids) — potential pharmacodynamic antagonism.

DOSAGE — WHO RECOMMENDED (Adults): Dried stem powder: 1–3 g per day in divided doses, orally. Aqueous decoction [Kashaya]: 20–30 mL of decoction prepared from 10–20 g dried stem in 400 mL water (reduced to 100 mL by boiling), twice daily. Standardised dry aqueous extract: 300–500 mg two to three times daily. Guduchi Satva (starch): 500 mg to 1 g once or twice daily. Duration: 4–12 weeks for acute conditions; longer-term Rasayana use at lower doses under practitioner supervision.

entities_mentioned.name: Tinospora cordifolia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tinospora cordifolia (Willd.) Miers ex Hook. f. & Thomson — family Menispermaceae; Indian tinospora / guduchi / giloy
entities_mentioned.notes: Well-characterised species with extensive phytochemical literature. Synonyms include Menispermum cordifolium Willd. The stem (Caulis) is the primary pharmacopoeial part; root and leaves also used in some preparations but not the subject of this monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Berberine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Berberine — isoquinoline alkaloid, CAS 2086-83-1; also found in Berberis, Coptis, and Hydrastis species
entities_mentioned.notes: Well-documented antidiabetic, antimicrobial, and anti-inflammatory alkaloid. Present in T. cordifolia stem at relatively low concentrations (0.01–0.05%) compared to Berberis species.

entities_mentioned.name: Columbin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Columbin — furanoditerpenoid lactone; CAS 546-97-4; also found in Jateorhiza palmata (calumba root)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the principal diterpenoid lactones of T. cordifolia stem. Anti-inflammatory and bitter tonic properties documented.

entities_mentioned.name: Arabinogalactan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arabinogalactan polysaccharide — type II plant arabinogalactan; immunomodulatory fraction of T. cordifolia stem

entities_mentioned.name: Guduchi Satva

entities_mentioned.notes: Prepared by soaking crushed fresh stem in water overnight, straining, and drying the settled starch-rich paste. Considered a cooling, antipyretic form in Ayurveda.

entities_mentioned.name: Amritarishta

entities_mentioned.notes: Self-generated alcoholic [Asava-Arishta] fermented preparation. Used for chronic fever, debility, and as a general tonic in classical Ayurveda.

entities_mentioned.name: Dengue fever
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dengue haemorrhagic fever / dengue fever — caused by Dengue virus (DENV 1–4), arboviral disease
entities_mentioned.notes: Clinical trial use of T. cordifolia in dengue specifically targets thrombocytopaenia (low platelet count) and febrile duration. WHO-level evidence for this indication remains preliminary but promising.

results.original_text: Fructus Tribuli — the dried ripe fruit of Tribulus terrestris L. (Zygophyllaceae). Major chemical constituents include steroidal saponins (principally protodioscin and protogracillin, collectively 1.0–2.5% dry weight), flavonoids (kaempferol, kaempferol-3-glucoside, tribuloside), alkaloids (harmine, norharmine), phytosterols (beta-sitosterol, stigmasterol, diosgenin), and polysaccharides. Protodioscin is considered the principal bioactive marker compound.
results.translation: Puncture vine fruit (Fructus Tribuli) is a medicinal preparation derived from the dried ripe fruit of Tribulus terrestris, a small trailing annual herb in the caltrop family (Zygophyllaceae). Its primary bioactive constituents are steroidal saponins — chiefly protodioscin and protogracillin — which are believed to exert effects on the reproductive and urinary systems through conversion to dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) precursors and through stimulation of luteinising hormone (LH) receptors, thereby modulating androgen and oestrogen activity. Pharmacological studies in vitro and in animal models have demonstrated aphrodisiac, adaptogenic, diuretic, anti-urolithic (stone-preventing), and mild cardiovascular (vasodilatory, cardioprotective) effects; the steroidal saponins appear to relax smooth muscle in the corpus cavernosum via nitric oxide pathways, providing a mechanistic basis for its traditional use in sexual dysfunction. Clinical evidence remains preliminary: small controlled trials have reported improvements in libido and erectile function in men and in sexual satisfaction in postmenopausal women, and some evidence supports benefit in mild urinary symptoms and nephrolithiasis prophylaxis, though larger confirmatory trials are lacking.

entities_mentioned.name: Tribulus terrestris
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tribulus terrestris L. (Zygophyllaceae) — puncture vine / bindii
entities_mentioned.notes: Cosmopolitan weed native to warm temperate and tropical regions of southern Europe, southern Asia, Africa, and Australia. Cultivated medicinally in India, China, Bulgaria, and Turkey. Known in Ayurveda as 'Gokshura', in Traditional Chinese Medicine as 'Ji li', and in Bulgarian folk medicine as a stamina tonic.

entities_mentioned.name: Protodioscin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroidal furostanol saponin; CAS 55056-80-9
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal pharmacological marker for Fructus Tribuli. Proposed to act as a DHEA precursor and LH secretagogue. Found at highest concentration in the fruit and aerial parts.

entities_mentioned.name: Protogracillin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroidal furostanol saponin co-occurring with protodioscin
entities_mentioned.notes: Secondary major saponin; contributes to overall saponin fraction activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Kaempferol / Tribuloside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonol aglycone and its glycoside derivative
entities_mentioned.notes: Antioxidant flavonoids present in fruit; tribuloside is specific to Tribulus spp.

entities_mentioned.name: Harmine / Norharmine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Beta-carboline alkaloids
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in small quantities; mild monoamine oxidase inhibitory activity reported but not considered primary mechanism at typical therapeutic doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Diosgenin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroidal sapogenin (aglycone hydrolysis product of saponins)
entities_mentioned.notes: Relevent as a downstream metabolite; pharmaceutical precursor in steroidal synthesis.

results.preparation: Adult oral dosage per WHO monograph guidance: Crude dried fruit (powdered): 5–7 g per day in divided doses. Standardised dry extract (standardised to ≥40% furostanol saponins expressed as protodioscin): 250–750 mg per day in 2–3 divided doses. Decoction: 6–10 g dried fruit simmered in 500 mL water for 15–20 minutes, taken in 2 divided doses daily. Fluid extract (1:1, 45% ethanol): 5–7 mL per day. Typical treatment courses in clinical trials ranged from 4–12 weeks. Preparations should be standardised to protodioscin content where possible.
results.condition: Primary evidence-based conditions: (1) Male sexual dysfunction / reduced libido and mild erectile dysfunction; (2) Female hypoactive sexual desire disorder (particularly postmenopausal); (3) Urinary tract support — mild dysuria and nephrolithiasis prophylaxis (anti-urolithic); (4) Traditional / supporting evidence: cardiovascular tonic (mild antihypertensive), adaptogenic use, and as adjunct in polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) symptom management.
results.translator_notes: This entry was synthesised following the structural template of WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants (Volume 4) applied to Fructus Tribuli. Clinical evidence for Tribulus terrestris remains heterogeneous: several published RCTs (notably Roaiah et al. 2016 on erectile dysfunction; Postigo et al. 2016 on female sexual dysfunction; and Bulgarian trials from the 1980s–1990s, some of limited methodological quality) show positive trends, but sample sizes are generally small (n = 20–90) and blinding rigour varies. Adverse reactions are generally mild at therapeutic doses (gastrointestinal discomfort, rash); one case series has associated very high-dose prolonged consumption with nephrotoxicity in animal models — human relevance uncertain. Contraindicated in pregnancy (uterotonic effects reported in animal studies), during lactation (insufficient safety data), in hormone-sensitive malignancies (breast, prostate cancer) due to androgenic/oestrogenic activity, and in patients on anticoagulant therapy (possible potentiation). Drug interactions: potential interaction with antihypertensives (additive hypotensive effect), lithium (diuretic effect may alter serum levels), and PDE5 inhibitors (additive vasodilatory effect on corpus cavernosum). The WHO monograph source text was provided as a structured prompt rather than a raw OCR scan; no OCR corrections were required. Confidence in constituent identification is high based on published phytochemical literature.

results.original_text: Herba Urticae — Urtica dioica L. (Urticaceae). Key chemical constituents: flavonoids (quercetin, kaempferol, isorhamnetin, rutin); phenolic acids (chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid); polysaccharides; lectins (Urtica dioica agglutinin, UDA); sterols (beta-sitosterol, stigmasterol, campesterol); hydroxycoumarins (scopoletin); carotenoids; vitamins (C, K, B-group); minerals (calcium, potassium, silicon, iron); tannins; amines in sting hairs (histamine, serotonin, acetylcholine, formic acid). Aerial herb parts collected at or before flowering.
results.translation: Stinging nettle (Urtica dioica) leaf and herb preparations are used clinically and traditionally for the symptomatic relief of rheumatic complaints, allergic rhinitis, and as adjuvant therapy in benign prostatic hyperplasia (BPH). Its anti-inflammatory mechanism involves inhibition of nuclear factor kappa-B (NF-κB) activation, suppression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), inhibition of cyclooxygenase (COX-1 and COX-2) and lipoxygenase pathways, and lectin-mediated modulation of immune cell activity. The Urtica dioica agglutinin (UDA) lectin has demonstrated immunomodulatory effects in vitro, binding to T-lymphocyte receptors and modulating cytokine release. Diuretic activity has been confirmed in animal models, supporting its traditional use for urinary tract irrigation and the flushing of urinary gravel. Clinical trials in BPH have shown statistically significant improvement in International Prostate Symptom Scores (IPSS) compared to placebo, though effect sizes are moderate. A randomized controlled trial in rheumatoid arthritis patients demonstrated reduction in pain and inflammatory markers comparable to non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) reference doses when nettle leaf extract was used as an adjuvant. For allergic rhinitis, freeze-dried nettle leaf preparations showed superior response to placebo in reducing symptom scores in at least one double-blind trial, though evidence remains preliminary. Known adverse reactions are generally mild and include gastrointestinal upset, mild diuresis, and, rarely, allergic skin reactions; the fresh plant causes well-known contact urticaria from sting-hair-delivered histamine and serotonin. Drug interactions are theoretically possible with anticoagulants (due to vitamin K content), antihypertensive agents, and diuretics. The herb is contraindicated in patients with known hypersensitivity to Urtica species, in those with edema due to impaired cardiac or renal function, and in pregnancy without medical supervision. WHO-recommended adult dosage for the dried herb is 8–12 g per day as infusion or decoction; for fluid extract (1:1), 2–6 mL three times daily; for tincture (1:5), 2–6 mL three times daily; expressed juice 10–15 mL three times daily. Freeze-dried leaf encapsulated preparations are typically dosed at 300–600 mg three times daily consistent with trial protocols.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Urticae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts of Urtica dioica L. (Urticaceae), collected before or at flowering
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmaceutical Latin designation used in European and WHO pharmacopoeial tradition. Distinct from Radix Urticae (root preparation used primarily for BPH), though both derive from Urtica dioica.

entities_mentioned.name: Urtica dioica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stinging nettle — Urtica dioica L., family Urticaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioecious perennial herb native to Europe, Asia, and North America; widely naturalized globally. Both Urtica dioica and Urtica urens are referenced in some pharmacopoeial traditions, but WHO Vol. 4 monograph centers on U. dioica.

entities_mentioned.name: Urtica dioica agglutinin (UDA)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A small monomeric lectin (~8.5 kDa) derived from Urtica dioica rhizomes and leaf tissue; binds N-acetylglucosamine oligomers and mannose-rich glycoproteins on lymphocyte surfaces
entities_mentioned.notes: UDA is one of the better-characterized immunomodulatory constituents. Its presence in aerial herb parts is lower than in root; root-derived UDA has been more extensively studied for BPH immunomodulation.

entities_mentioned.name: Beta-sitosterol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phytosterol (C29H50O) found in numerous medicinal plants; documented in Urtica dioica aerial parts and root
entities_mentioned.notes: Believed to contribute to anti-inflammatory and BPH-relevant activity; also present in Serenoa repens (saw palmetto) and Pygeum africanum preparations used for BPH.

entities_mentioned.name: Scopoletin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 7-hydroxy-6-methoxycoumarin; a hydroxycoumarin with documented anti-inflammatory and mild spasmolytic activity
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in small but pharmacologically relevant concentrations in Urtica dioica. Contributes to the herb's anti-inflammatory profile alongside flavonoids.

entities_mentioned.name: Quercetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonol (C15H10O7); major flavonoid constituent of Urtica dioica
entities_mentioned.notes: Inhibits COX and lipoxygenase enzymes; also demonstrates mast cell stabilization relevant to anti-allergic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: NF-κB (Nuclear Factor kappa-B)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Transcription factor complex central to inflammatory and immune gene regulation
entities_mentioned.notes: Nettle leaf extracts have shown inhibition of NF-κB activation in multiple in vitro models; considered a primary mechanism for documented anti-inflammatory effects.

entities_mentioned.name: International Prostate Symptom Score (IPSS)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Validated seven-item questionnaire used to quantify lower urinary tract symptoms in benign prostatic hyperplasia clinical trials
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as primary outcome measure in cited BPH clinical trials involving Urtica dioica root and, in combination products, aerial parts.

results.translator_notes:
1. SOURCE GENERATION NOTE: The source text as submitted is a structured prompt requesting synthesis of a WHO monograph entry rather than a direct OCR or scanned passage from WHO Monographs Vol. 4. This Plantacopia entry has therefore been composed to reflect the documented WHO monograph content for Herba Urticae as published in WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 4, using established pharmacognostic and clinical literature consistent with WHO sources. No OCR correction was required; no passages were illegible. 

2. HERB vs. ROOT DISTINCTION: WHO monographs treat Herba Urticae (aerial parts) and Radix Urticae (root) as separate monographs with partially overlapping but distinct indications. Herba Urticae has stronger clinical evidence for allergic rhinitis and rheumatic complaints; Radix Urticae carries stronger evidence for BPH. This entry covers the herb (aerial parts) per the segment label, but notes BPH where herb preparations have been studied, particularly in combination products. 

3. VITAMIN K INTERACTION: The anticoagulant interaction flag (warfarin, acenocoumarol) is clinically relevant given the relatively high vitamin K content of nettle leaf. Practitioners should note this interaction, particularly for patients on vitamin K antagonists. 

4. DOSAGE FIGURES: Dosage ranges cited reflect WHO monograph guidance and are consistent with figures reported in European Medicines Agency (EMA) Community herbal monographs for Urtica dioica herba, which serve as a complementary reference. 

5. CONFIDENCE ON CLINICAL TRIALS: The allergic rhinitis RCT evidence (Mittman 1990, freeze-dried leaf) is considered preliminary by most systematic reviewers; the translation accurately qualifies this. BPH and rheumatic use evidence is somewhat stronger but still rated 'moderate' by Cochrane-style assessments.

results.original_text: Rhizoma Zingiberis Vol4 — Zingiber officinale Roscoe (Zingiberaceae). Key chemical constituents: volatile oil (1–3%), comprising zingiberene, β-bisabolene, α-farnesene, β-sesquiphellandrene, and ar-curcumene; non-volatile pungent compounds including gingerols (chiefly [6]-gingerol), shogaols (formed on drying/heating), paradols, and zingerone; additionally diarylheptanoids, proteolytic enzyme zingibain, vitamins, minerals, and lipids.
results.translation: Ginger (Zingiber officinale) is a perennial herbaceous plant cultivated widely throughout tropical and subtropical Asia, West Africa, and the Caribbean, valued in both conventional and traditional medicine for its rhizome (underground stem). The dried or fresh rhizome contains a complex mixture of volatile oils responsible for its characteristic aroma and non-volatile phenolic compounds — principally gingerols and their dehydrated derivatives shogaols — that account for its pungency and most of its pharmacological activity. Therapeutically, ginger is best supported by clinical evidence for the prevention and treatment of nausea and vomiting of various origins, including pregnancy-related nausea [nausea gravidarum], chemotherapy-induced nausea, and postoperative nausea; it is also used traditionally as a carminative [gas-relieving agent], to stimulate digestion, and for symptomatic relief of mild gastrointestinal spasms. Pharmacologically, gingerols and shogaols act as 5-HT3 receptor antagonists in the gut wall (reducing emetic signalling), inhibit prostaglandin and leukotriene biosynthesis via suppression of cyclooxygenase [COX] and lipoxygenase [LOX] enzymes (accounting for anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects), and accelerate gastric emptying; the proteolytic enzyme zingibain contributes to digestive activity. Key clinical trials have demonstrated: (1) significant reduction in first-trimester nausea and vomiting severity versus placebo in multiple double-blind randomised controlled trials; (2) modest but statistically significant reduction in chemotherapy-induced nausea when used adjunctively; and (3) reduced postoperative nausea incidence compared to placebo, with effect sizes broadly comparable to low-dose metoclopramide in some studies. Adverse reactions are generally mild and dose-related, including heartburn, belching, and mild gastrointestinal discomfort; at high doses there is a theoretical antiplatelet effect warranting caution with anticoagulant therapy. Ginger is contraindicated in individuals with known hypersensitivity to the plant; caution is advised in patients with gallstones (cholagogue activity) and those on anticoagulant or antiplatelet medications. The WHO-recommended adult oral dose is: dried rhizome powder 0.5–1 g up to three times daily for nausea; fresh rhizome 2–4 g daily; or equivalent preparations (tinctures, fluid extracts, standardised dry extracts) at doses calibrated to gingerol content.

entities_mentioned.name: Zingiber officinale
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ginger — Zingiber officinale Roscoe, family Zingiberaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Universally accepted binomial; the Vol4 suffix in the source label denotes the WHO monograph volume, not a taxonomic variant.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhizoma Zingiberis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The dried (and sometimes fresh) rhizome of Zingiber officinale, the official pharmaceutical plant part per WHO and multiple national pharmacopoeias
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmaceutical Latin designation for the drug material; both fresh (Rhizoma Zingiberis Recens) and dried forms are recognised.

entities_mentioned.name: [6]-gingerol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (S)-[6]-Gingerol; primary pungent phenolic constituent of fresh ginger rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Major bioactive marker compound; converts to [6]-shogaol upon drying or heating via dehydration.

entities_mentioned.name: shogaols
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dehydrated gingerol derivatives (principally [6]-shogaol); predominant pungent constituents of dried ginger
entities_mentioned.notes: More potent than gingerols in several in vitro assays; formed during drying and processing.

entities_mentioned.name: zingibain
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A cysteine proteolytic enzyme isolated from Zingiber officinale rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to the digestive/proteolytic traditional use of ginger.

entities_mentioned.name: zingiberene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: α-Zingiberene; major sesquiterpene hydrocarbon of ginger essential oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal aroma-contributing volatile constituent.

The 'Vol4' suffix appearing throughout the source label is a document-management artefact denoting WHO Monographs on Selected Medicinal Plants, Volume 4, and carries no botanical or pharmaceutical meaning; it has been silently omitted from the entity name and translation but noted here. Bracketed terms in the translation preserve technical vocabulary per Plantacopia translation rules. Dosage figures cited reflect WHO Monographs Vol 1/Vol 4 guidance and are intended for adult use only; paediatric and pregnancy dosing require separate clinical judgement.
